cards.text: The Pole Star appears stationary in the North direction, which helps to locate the North direction in the Northern hemisphere.

Nubra Region Characteristics
  • Nubra is a breathtaking area located in Ladakh.
  • Yangdol and her twin brother Dorjay live in this picturesque region.
  • The weather is largely cloudless.

Night Sky in Nubra
  • The night sky is brilliantly lit with stars due to minimal air and light pollution.
  • Yangdol and Dorjay find awe in the clear, starry nights.

Stories of the Stars
  • Elders tell tales of how stars have aided caravans in direction finding.
  • Yangdol and Dorjay wonder about the size and distance of stars.
  • They enjoy identifying patterns among the stars.

Characteristics of Stars
  • Stars vary in brightness, with some shining brighter than others.
  • Stars emit their own light, forming recognizable patterns.

Historical Star Patterns
  • Ancestors saw star patterns as animals, objects, or characters from stories.
  • These shapes made identifying stars easier.

Uses of Star Patterns
  • Star patterns were vital for navigation before modern technology.
  • They served as guides for sailors and travelers.

Constellations Defined
  • Originally groups of stars forming patterns, currently sky regions are called constellations.
  • The term 'constellation' remains popular for these star groups.

International Constellation Boundaries
  • The IAU established boundaries for constellations in the early 20th century.
  • There are 88 officially listed constellations dividing the sky.

Orion Constellation
  • Orion is viewed as a hunter with three stars forming his belt.
  • The constellation is intricately related to other star groups like Canis Major and Taurus.

Imaginary Lines in Star Patterns
  • Fig. 12.3 showcases constellations with red and green lines for easier recognition.
  • These lines are fictional and serve identification purposes.

Nakṣhatra in Indian Astronomy
  • A term signifying stars or star groups, e.g., Ārdrā and Kṛittikā.
  • Aldebaran in Taurus is known as Rohiṇī.

Patterns of Dipper Constellations
  • Two dipper patterns, the Big Dipper and Little Dipper, are prominent.
  • Little Dipper features the North-indicating Pole Star.

Direction with Pole Star
  • The Pole Star is seen as fixed in the North.
  • Useful for navigation in the Northern hemisphere.

Indian Names for Constellations
  • The Big Dipper is called Saptaṛiṣhi.
  • The Pole Star is known as Dhruva tārā.

Regional Star Stories
  • Tribes in India have unique tales involving stars.
  • For instance, the Big Dipper stars symbolize a "grandmother's cot" or a "boat".

Star Visibility
  • Stars are visible on clear, cloudless nights.
  • Visibility is reduced in big cities due to light pollution, smoke, and dust.

Light Pollution
  • Excessive artificial light at night causes light pollution.
  • Light pollution affects visibility of stars.

Urban vs Rural Areas
  • Fewer stars are visible in cities.
  • More stars can be seen in villages with less light pollution.

Obstruction and Viewing
  • Tall buildings and trees can block views of the sky.
  • The best observation spots are open dark areas.

Global Light Pollution Trends
  • Light pollution is increasing worldwide.
  • This limits star and celestial object observation.

Dark Sky Reserves
  • Established to control light pollution.
  • Preserve darkness for study and enjoyment of night skies.

Visibility of Stars
  • Star and constellation visibility varies by location and time.
  • Pole Star is not visible from the southern hemisphere.

Identifying Stars and Constellations
  • Know the appearance and location of constellations.
  • Use images or apps to learn star patterns.

Using Technology
  • Sky mapping apps help identify stars and constellations.
  • "Sky Map" and "Stellarium" are helpful tools.

Preparation for Night Sky Watching
  • Choose a dark, open area away from obstruction and lights.
  • Plan observation on moonless, cloudless nights.

Using Apps and Aids
  • Use sky maps and printed constellation images.
  • A compass and notebook can be useful for navigation and recording.

Timing and Environment
  • The selected date and time should align with observation goals.
  • Visit the location when conditions are optimal for viewing.

Arrival and Adjustment
  • Accompany an adult to the observation site.
  • Allow your eyes to adjust to the dark for better visibility.

Tools for Observation
  • Bring technological aids like apps for ease of identification.
  • Use traditional tools like a magnetic compass if needed.

Enjoying the Night Sky
  • Engage in night sky watching with preparedness and awareness.
  • Preserve darkness to enrich the viewing experience.
The Sun is a star. It is the star closest to us. It is an extremely hot spherical ball of gases. The Sun gives out a huge amount of energy, and that is why it glows so brightly.
The Sun produces heat and light, and is the main source of energy on the Earth.
A planet is a large, nearly spherical object that revolves around the sun. Our earth is a planet as it revolves around the sun.
The eight planets, in order of their increasing distance from the Sun, are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.
The inner four planets nearest to the Sun - Mercury, Venus, Earth and Mars - are smaller in size. They have solid surfaces with rocks on them.
Venus is usually seen shining brightly at dawn and dusk, and is commonly called the Morning Star or the Evening Star, even though it is not a star.
Mars is called the Red Planet because it appears red. This is because the soil on Mars is reddish in colour.
A large portion of the Earth’s surface is covered with water and thus, it appears blue from the space. Due to this, the Earth is also called the Blue Planet.
Objects that move around planets are commonly called satellites. They are smaller in size than planets. Moons are natural satellites of planets. The Earth has one Moon.
Earth’s natural satellite, the Moon, takes about 27 days to complete one revolution around the Earth. It is our nearest neighbour in the space.
The Sun and the planets are nearly spherical in shape. There are many small objects in the Solar System which are rocky and irregular in shape. These are called asteroids.
Sometimes we have visitors from the outer regions of the Solar System! These objects with long tails are called comets. They are made up of dust, gases, rocks and ice.
The Sun The Sun is a star and the closest one to Earth. It appears small due to its vast distance even though it is much larger than our planet.
The Sun's Distance The Sun is approximately 150 million kilometers away from Earth, also known as one astronomical unit (au).
The Sun's Role Being the main energy source, the Sun affects Earth's climate, seasons, weather, and sustains life, allowing plants to produce essential oxygen.
Comparing Stars Other stars are much further than the Sun, making them appear as small points compared to the relatively larger Sun.
Proxima Centauri The nearest star to us after the Sun is Proxima Centauri, located about 269,000 astronomical units away.
Planets Planets are large, nearly spherical objects revolving around the Sun. Our Earth is one such planet.
The Earth's Movements While revolving around the Sun, Earth also rotates on its axis, taking approximately 24 hours for a full rotation, defining a day.

Order of Planets The eight planets by increasing distance from the Sun:
  • Mercury
  • Venus
  • Earth
  • Mars
  • Jupiter
  • Saturn
  • Uranus
  • Neptune
Inner and Outer Planets The inner planets, Mercury to Mars, have solid surfaces, whereas outer planets, Jupiter to Neptune, are gaseous with ring structures.
Dwarf Planets Pluto is now classified as a dwarf planet, as per the International Astronomical Union's definition. These objects are smaller than traditional planets.
Understanding Satellites Natural satellites orbit around planets; Earth has one moon, while other planets, like Jupiter, have numerous moons.
The Moon Our Moon is about 384,000 km away, completing a revolution around Earth every 27 days. It has craters formed from space objects impacts.
Exploring the Moon India's Chandrayaan missions have significantly contributed to Moon exploration, with the third mission achieving a historic landing at its south pole.
Asteroids Asteroids are rocky, irregular-shaped objects, primarily orbiting between Mars and Jupiter, forming the asteroid belt.
Comets Originating from the Solar System's outer regions, comets feature distinctive tails formed as they approach the Sun, due to melting ice and dust.
Our home galaxy called the Milky Way Galaxy or Akasha Ganga. A galaxy has millions to billions of stars. Our Solar System is part of the Milky Way Galaxy.

Milky Way Visibility
On moonless nights, away from city lights, the Milky Way forms a faint band across the sky from north to south.

The Milky Way Name
The alternate name for the Milky Way Galaxy is Ākāśha Gangā.

Milky Way Composition
The Milky Way contains millions to billions of stars.

Location of Our Solar System
Our Solar System is a part of the Milky Way Galaxy.

Night Sky Conditions
For best visibility of the Milky Way, observe from dark places away from city lights.

Direction of the Milky Way
The Milky Way is visible from near north to south across the night sky.

Galaxy Definition
A galaxy is a system containing millions to billions of stars, gas, and dust.

Milky Way Structure
The Milky Way forms a distinctive band of light due to its billions of stars.

Darkness and Visibility
To see celestial features like the Milky Way, reduced light pollution is essential.

Composition of Stars
Stars within the Milky Way vary in size and brightness, contributing to its glow.

Why Moonless Nights?
Moonless nights enhance the visibility of dim celestial objects like galaxies.

Galactic Positioning
The Milky Way's position stretches bow-like across the sky.

Observing Milky Way
Observers can see the Milky Way's light as a faint band stretching across the sky.

Cross-Sky Phenomenon
The Milky Way spans multiple constellations viewed as one continuous band.

City Light Impact
Cities, with their bright lights, obscure the Milky Way's presence in the sky.
Galaxies There are many galaxies in outer space beyond the Milky Way Galaxy.
Purpose of Studying Galaxies Scientists study galaxies to understand stars, galaxies, and the universe.
Search for Extraterrestrial Life Whether life exists elsewhere in the universe remains unknown.
Exoplanets The search for life largely targets exoplanets, which orbit other stars in our galaxy.
Life on Exoplanets Scientists have not yet found evidence of life on exoplanets, but the search continues.

Constellations
  • The sky is divided into regions called constellations.
  • Constellations are groups of stars forming patterns.
Pole Star The Pole Star appears stationary and helps locate the North direction in the Northern hemisphere.
Nature of the Sun The Sun is a star that produces heat and light.
Definition of a Planet A planet is a large, nearly spherical object that revolves around the Sun.

Order of Planets
  • Mercury
  • Venus
  • Earth
  • Mars
  • Jupiter
  • Saturn
  • Uranus
  • Neptune
Planets listed in order of increasing distance from the Sun.
Earth's Orbit The Earth revolves around the Sun in nearly one year.
Satellites Objects that move around planets are called satellites.

The Moon
  • The Moon is Earth's natural satellite.
  • It revolves around the Earth in nearly 27 days.
Composition of the Solar System The Sun, eight planets, their moons, asteroids, and comets form the Solar System.
Milky Way Membership Our Solar System is part of the Milky Way Galaxy.
The sky is divided into regions called constellations, which include groups of stars that appear to form patterns.
The Pole Star appears stationary in the North direction, which helps in locating the North direction in the Northern hemisphere.
The Sun is a star which produces heat and light.
A planet is a large, nearly spherical object that revolves around the Sun.
Objects that move around planets are commonly called satellites.
